title: ECE  Program Review 2018 19 (Part 1)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Instructional Program Review 2017-2018 12/15/2018
• Child Development and Early Education (ECE)
• Patrice Thatcher-Stephens, Program Coordinator Child Development and Early
Education, ECE
1. Description of Program:
a) Organization Staffing and Structure:
The ECE—Child Development and Early Education Program is organized as a Career and Technical
Education Program under the purview of the Dean of CTE as follows
• President/Superintendent of College of the Siskiyous
• Vice President of Instruction
• Dean CTE
• ECE Program Coordinator and Full Time Tenured Instructor
• Experienced and fully qualified Adjuncts hired on semester by semester basis
b) Primary Purpose:
The primary purpose of the ECE Program at College of the Siskiyous is preparation of front line early
educators for work throughout the geographical and expanded online area served by the college. Beyond that,
our program serves numerous other vital purposes within in the local and statewide early education and social
services community.
• The ECE--Child Development and Early Education Program at College has been part of the Career and
Technical Education Area at College of the Siskiyous since the mid 1960’s. The program has grown to
offer curriculum aligned with 109 other California Community Colleges throughout California.
• Graduates of our program find or advance in their existing employment at Head Start, California State
Preschools and in Independent Programs.
• Following the Two Year Course Rotation, students can complete an A.A. Degree in Child Development,
a Transfer Degree to any California State University or articulate, as a junior, to Southern Oregon
University’s online Early Childhood Development Program.
• Our Rotation of Courses offers classes leading to all levels of California State Child Development
Permits issues through the California State Department Commission on Teacher Credentialing.
• Our Rotation of Courses offers classes leading to all levels early educator qualifications included in
Title 22 Division 12 from the California State Department of Social Services Community Care Licensing,
Teacher Qualifications.
• As online degrees have become more accessible, COS, students are currently pursuing the Bachelor’s
Degrees at National University, Ashford University, Simpson College and Pacific Oaks.
• Many students choose to complete their B.A. degrees in elementary or primary education
• Students often work in early education programs while completing classes for their A.S. degree,
Permits, or transfer requirements.
• Our practicum site at Discovery Child Development Center--a collaboration with Shasta Head Start--is
the only program in Siskiyou County Accredited by the National Association for the Education of Young
Children. Through our work with the California Early Childhood Mentor Teacher Program, we offer
additional practicum sites throughout Siskiyou County.
• Until July of 2018 the ECE Program oversaw the Foster and Kinship Care Education Program, a CCCCO
designated funding initiative. Under our leadership, this program was recognized as a model of
community and statewide collaboration in serving families offering foster care education to
underserved families in Siskiyou County.

title: ECE  Program Review 2018 19 (Part 3)
text:
c) Whom you serve (including demographics)
Geographic Service Area
Siskiyou County, adjacent to Oregon in the northern, inland region of California, is the fifth largest
county in the state. More than sixty percent of Siskiyou County’s land is managed by federal and state
government agencies. These include the USDA Forest Service, the Bureau of Land Management, the U.S. Fish
and Wildlife Service, and the California Department of Fish and Game. The county’s 6,347 square miles
encompass a wide variety of geographic areas and community structures.
Actual Students Served
The ECE Program at College of the Siskiyous serves students, literally throughout this area through our
videoconference capabilities. Beyond the two main campus sites—Week and Yreka--Video conference sites
have included at various times
• McCloud
• Dunsmuir
• Butte Valley
• Happy Camp
• Scott Valley
• Tule Lake
• Modoc County
Although the actual numbers, i.e. FTES, represented at each of these far distant sites is miniscule, the
effects of out preparation is reflected in early educator preparation throughout the region
Siskiyou County is one of the most sparsely populated counties in California. Our students are spread
throughout an area averaging approximately seven persons per square mile. Our students are often
geographically isolated and served through videoconference sites throughout the county as well as at the
main Campus in Weed and our site in Yreka. Our ECE students are both service providers and clients of family
support services. The primary employers of College of the Siskiyous Early Childhood Education students are
the California State Preschool, as administered through various entities, Head Start and Early Head Start, as
administered through four different entities and a few local independent early education programs. We also
serve family childcare providers.
Our students range in age and demographics from recent high school graduates living in the on-
campus dorms to experienced early educators near retirement age who are challenged to meet the increasing
education requirements in our field. Our students are primarily female although we have a representative
number of males in our ECE and General Education Classes. In spring 2019 we will have our first male graduate
in recent memory. We collaborate effectively with Disabled Student Services to serve eligible students with a
wide variety of special needs ranging from cerebral palsy to dyslexia.
Ethnicity
Based on data from the Annie E. Casey Foundation “Kids County Data Center” and most recent census
data, the total population of Siskiyou County is approximately 45,000. The child population is approximately
10,000. The overall ethnicity of Siskiyou County is 87% European Descent, 10% Hispanic, 3% Native American,
1.3% Asian American, 1.2% African American, 6.1% Mixed, 1.2% ‘other’ (Yes, this adds up to over 100%--just
referring to data…). Based on 2016 data, distribution of children, 0-5, of European Descent in Siskiyou County
is 65%, the percentage of the 0-5 population that is Hispanic is 20%, Native American is 10%, African American
is 1.5%, Asian or Pacific Islander 1% and “Other” 8% (again over 100%).

title: ECE  Program Review 2018 19 (Part 4)
text:
-- 2 of 21 --

The significant ‘take-away’ from official, albeit ‘sketchy’ (that adding up to over 100% thing…) public
data is that, in every case, the percentage of the general non-European child population of Siskiyou County
exceeds the adult percentages. Per the PIVOT Data from our college research office, our program appears to
reflect these trends, although college students have not been encouraged to accurately respond to
demographic questions. This alignment is undoubtedly is coincident with the determination of early
education programs to recruit and employ educators who accurately represent the ethnic make-up of the
families they serve as well as the relative youth of COS students in general.
Socioeconomic Status and Generational Poverty
Lack of higher education opportunity is endemic in rural America and affects both the employment
opportunities and life experiences of our students. This is compounded by regional transportation challenges
and the challenges of our climate zone. The College of the Siskiyous is a lifeline to county residents. The ECE
Program’s Video Conferencing, and to a lesser extend online, capability is essential to both the well-being of
the current generation of students and the future of the county. While some students struggle to enroll in,
complete classes, and pursue additional classes required for Child Development Permits and graduation a
substantial number complete local degrees, transfer degrees, and obtain permits. Program data, verified by
Child Development Training Consortium Student Profiles submitted to state and federal funding agencies each
semester, documents from 10 to 20 students consistently enrolled in classes leading to Child Development
Permits. The CDTC numbers reflect only students employed in early education programs while COS.
d and e) Program Services and Methods of Delivery
• We do not provide any Noncredit Courses.
o Non-credit classes do not meet the academic, permit and transfer needs, of our students.
• We do not provide Basic Skills Courses.
o Basic Skills classes do not meet the academic, permit and transfer needs, of our students.
o We do value COS’s Basic Skills preparation and these courses support out students’ success.
Through the CDTC subsidy program (see below) we award a cash stipend to qualifying students
for Basic Skills units.
• Degree Applicable, Transfer Level and AD-T Classes Face-to-Face/Videoconferenced/Online
o Local A.A. Degrees
o AD-T Degrees
o Child Development Permits at all levels
 MOI: Face-to-Face/Videoconferenced/Online
 Delivered through the Instruction Office and
• Supplemental academic support relating to Counseling/Advising
 Delivered through consistent and collaborative work with COS academic advisors and
counselors.

-- 3 of 21 --

title: ECE  Program Review 2018 19 (Part 5)
text:
 Through our consistent Two Year Course Rotation and our ECE @COS Program Guide we
offer students a clear road map to meeting their educational and professional goals.
• We provide exemplary support for students with Special Needs and Disabilities.
 Delivered through collaboration with COS’ DSPS program. Thanks!
• Program Coordinator works with ECE Curriculum Alignment Project Work group and monitors CAP
alignment at College of the Siskiyous. This initiative led directly to our early approval as an ECE AD-T
degree through the CCCCO.
 Delivered through statewide collaboration with early education partners.
• Student-Centered enrollment tracking and class scheduling.
 Delivered through consistent monitoring and student survey.
• Siskiyous/ Shasta Head Start collaboration that allows College of the Siskiyous to have an onsite
Practicum site at Discovery Child Development Center.
 Delivered through the establishment of an ongoing COS MOU with Shasta Head Start
• Program Coordinator serves on California early Childhood Mentor Teacher team, working to provide
practicum sites throughout the county.
 Delivered through site evaluations and Mentor/Director Program assessment.
• The ECE Program redesigned the failing Foster Kinship Care Education Program at College of the
Siskiyous beginning in 2012 to meet CCCCO standards and meet community needs.
 Delivered through FKCE embedded in ECE Program 2012—2018.
• Program Director acts as the campus liaison to Child Development Training Consortium making CDTC
support available to COS ECE students.
 Delivered through
 Assessment of CHILD Development Permit applications and determination of
qualification for CDTC reimbursement.
 Processing and submission of Student Profiles and determination of qualification for
CDTC unit subsidy.
 Attendance at regional and/or statewide consortium meetings as COS CDTC
Coordinator.
• Program Director represents College of the Siskiyous on the Siskiyou (formerly Siskiyou-Shasta) Quality
Rating Improvement System team.
 Delivered through collaboration with local partners to implement the QRIS finding through
which our county receives state and federal quality improvement funding for early education
programs.
• Schedule and organize twice-yearly ECE Advisory Committee Meetings.
• The Program Coordinator prepares and submits College of the Siskiyous ECE Program Annual Report to
Siskiyou early education network partners.
• The Program Coordinator designed and acted as Project Director for The Abacus Project a Ford Family
Foundation grant, funding an early math family initiative. This initiative has benefitted students directly
through curriculum enhancement and indirectly through materials and equipment provided to local
early education programs.

-- 4 of 21 --

Historic Context
The ECE Program has become more demanding over the past decade. Historic Success Rates were higher in
ECE classes throughout the CCC system prior to the full implementation of the Curriculum Alignment Project
after 2009. As the field required a more complete academic preparation, and California Community College
ECE programs committed to this higher standard, our COS courses went through a complete curriculum review
leading to both CAP alignment and our first wave AD-T degree.
At the same time, the ECE Program at COS became early adopters of online LMS’s, first ETUDES and now
Canvas. As in online education in general, we found that online students represented a different demographic
than Face-to-Face/Videoconference students.
We, oddly, took a little pride in the C+, slightly above ‘average’, success rates that our students achieved as we
first implemented this more rigorous curriculum and as we assumed the challenges of online instruction.
However, ever committed to continuous improvement, we also understood that, along with the more
challenging curriculum and new delivery method, we needed to offer new and better student preparation,
monitoring and support.
Indicators of Curriculum and Structure Updated Based on Student Need and Assessment Data include:
• Curriculum
o The ECE Program created a less cumbersome program with the decrease of degree units for the
local AS from 32 to 24. This unit requirement aligns more accurately with other CCC’s and with
the ECE Classes required for the AD-T Degree.
 This decrease in units increased our number of graduates with both local A.A. degrees
and ADT-T degrees.
 Since the change in required units, our graduation numbers have increased significantly.

-- 5 of 21 --

title: ECE  Program Review 2018 19 (Part 7)
text:
o Ongoing and Integrated SLO Assessment has allowed us to monitor and assess our program
ability to offer students class assessments and experiences reflecting course AD-T aligned SLO’s.
 Each year, for each class, a designated SLO is assessed aligning class assessments and
experiences with the designated SLO.
 Monitor the integration of classes that share similar SLO’s. As part of this process we:
o Design MOA’s that accurately assess student understanding at all levels of Bloom’s
Taxonomy.
o Scaffold and/or redirect students who might have failed in the past by carefully
monitoring individual student status at census.
 More accurate retention and success rates for ECE classes reflects this ability,
but we are still working on both scaffolding students who are struggling and
moving students to more developmentally appropriate classes before the
first census date to avoid the appearance and reality of late withdrawal and
failure.
o We have used ongoing and systematic SLO and PLO assessment to
 Alignment with statewide “CAP” and AD-T standards for ECE programs.
 Maintain and Align classes within our COS curriculum to reflect a common philosophy.
 Align classes within our COS curriculum to build upon a common knowledge base.
 Focus our work with students on both career goals (employment after completion) and
transfer goals (articulation and transfer rates).
g) Provide a breakdown of the classes offered in your areas within the Program Review cycle.
The Program Review Cycle at College of the Siskiyous has inconsistent over the past four years. As always, ECE, as a
Career and Technical Education programs has completed a program review every two years—however the
required format has been inconsistent. In the alternate years, we have used an Annual Update reflect on the
progress toward two-year goals; review our SLO assessment rotation and MOI’s.
Quite honestly, since COS’ our Planning Committee was reorganized in 2017, I have lost track of our ALO planning.
Our Continuous Quality Improvement Plan (CQIP) is inherent in our Annual Update review.
Year One:
ECE Classes by Semester Classes required for degrees are in BLUE
SLO Assessment for the 2016-2017 and 2017-18 focused on, aligned SLO’s 2 (2016) and 3 (2017) for all
classes. See COR’s.
Fall Classes
ECE 1001 Introduction to ECE Principles, Practices and Standards: This is the introductory class. It is a CORE class required
for the A.S./A.S.-T/Permits/Title 22 requirements. It is offered every fall face-to-face and videoconferenced. Very seldom it is offered
in the summer, online.
ECE 1002 Child Development: This is a CORE class required for the A.S./A.S.-T/Permits/Title 22 requirements. This class also
satisfies the G.E. Social Sciences requirement. It is offered every fall face-to-face and videoconferenced. It is also often offered
online, typically in the spring.
ECE 1003 Child, Family and Community: This is a CORE class required for the A.S./A.S.-T/Child Development Permits/Title 22
requirements. This class also satisfies the G.E. Social Sciences requirement. It is offered every fall face-to-face and videoconferenced.
It is not offered online.

-- 6 of 21 --

title: ECE  Program Review 2018 19 (Part 8)
text:
ECE 2000 Observing and Assessing--Desired Results and Developmentally Appropriate Environments:
This class is required for the A.S./A.S.-T/Advanced Permits. It is an advanced class—students should complete, or be
enrolled in, the majority of their CORE classes before enrolling in this class. It is offered every fall face-to-face and videoconferenced.
It is not offered online.
ECE 2002 Child Nutrition Health and Safety: This class is required for the A.S./A.S.-T/Advanced Permits. It is an advanced
class—students should complete, or be enrolled in, the majority of their CORE classes before enrolling in this class. It is offered every
fall face-to-face and videoconferenced. It is not offered online.
ECE 1999 Human Development: This class is NOT required for the A.S./A.S.-T/Child Development Permits. However, it does
satisfy a CORE class requirement (can replace ECE 1002) for the Child Development Permit. This class also satisfies the G.E. Social
Sciences requirement. It is offered most falls online and every spring face-to-face and videoconferenced.
ECE 2003 Serving Children and Families with Special Needs: This class is NOT required for the A.S./A.S.-T/satisfies Master
Teacher Permit specialization. It is offered in the fall online.
ECE 2527 Supervision of Adult Staff in Early Education: This class is NOT required for the A.S./A.S.-T/required for Site
Supervisor or Program Director Permits/required for Mentor Teachers. It is offered in the fall online.
ECE 0958 ECE Lab: This is a one or two unit lab-only class. This class is NOT required for the A.A./A.S.-T/required as one
extra lab unit for Southern Oregon University Articulation. It is offered most semesters.
Spring Classes
ECE 1500 Introduction to Curriculum: This is a CORE class required for the A.S./A.S.-T/Child Development Permits/Title 22
requirements. It is offered every fall face-to-face and videoconferenced. It is sometimes offered online, but is not regularly
scheduled as online.
ECE 1501 Positive Child Guidance: This class is NOT required for the A.S./A.S.-T/ applies to Master Teacher Permit
Specializations. It is offered in the spring online. 12
ECE 1502 Serving Children and Families in a Diverse Society: This is a class required for the A.S./A.S.-T/ applies to Master
Teacher Permit Specializations. This class also satisfies the G.E. Diversity requirement. It is offered every fall face-to-face and
videoconferenced. It is sometimes offered online, but is not regularly scheduled as online.
ECE 1999 Human Development: See Above
ECE 2500 Family Relationships and Parenting: This class is NOT required for the A.S./A.S.-T/ applies to Master Teacher
Permit Specializations. However, it does satisfy a CORE class requirement (can replace ECE 1003) for the Child Development
Permit. This class also satisfies the G.E. Diversity requirement. It is offered in the spring online.
ECE 2501 Administration: This class is NOT required for the A.S./A.S.-T/ required for Site Supervisor or Program Director
Permits/required for Mentor Teachers. It is offered in the spring online.
ECE 2502 Practicum: This is a class required for the A.S./A.S.-T/ Child Development Permits. This is a lecture/Lab class
requiring 6 per week hours of work in an approved early education program. The California State Department of Education and
Community Care Licensing Department of Social Services requires that students satisfy immunization requirements. See most
current Update on following pages.
ECE 2510 Creative Art Activities: This class is NOT required for the A.S./A.S.-T/ applies to Master Teacher Permit
Specializations. It is offered in the spring online.
ECE 0958 ECE Lab: See Above
2. External Factors with Significant Impact: What external factors have a significant impact on
your program?
a) Budgetary constraints or opportunities
The Child Development and Early Education, ECE, Program is an important program with both local and
statewide recognition and value. We are a relatively inexpensive program. Nevertheless, our experience has
been that the program is undervalued within the COS monetary structure. Quite frankly, I suspect that the
cost of a tenured faculty person as a Program coordinator is not fully appreciated as a benefit to the college
and community, and that our innovative and thrifty use of an aging temporary building as an exceptional lab
school, in collaboration with, and fully staffed by, Shasta Head Start is, likewise, under appreciated.

title: ECE  Program Review 2018 19 (Part 10)
text:
We place the direct monetary needs of students and DCDC first, with the majority of our costs
reflecting this perspective. Our minimal district funding is typically used exclusively to support the lab school,
Discovery Child Development Center and out students and classes who use that program as a clinical site.
Through strategic purchase of materials, we ensure that students can observe and practice concepts taught in
our classes.
We receive other student support funding through the Child Development Training Consortium, based
on units earned by students who are employed within the early education community and completing work
toward their Child Development Permits. Rather than redirecting this funding to administrative or materials
needs for the program, we consistently pay student the full CDTC subsidy to offset the cost of their education.
The grant funding which we have received is used for programs or initiatives which directly benefit
both COS students and the surrounding community.
In the past, we have received Perkins Funding through a Career and Technical Education area
administered funding request and award formula. This avenue of funding has been removed from availability
for the ECE Program over the past 5 years.
We do rather well with limited resources. With this Program Review, I will for the first time in many
years, include a budget request.
b) Competition from other institutions
We prefer to collaborate rather than compete. The majority of ECE programs and degrees at CCC’s
throughout California are aligned. Many have classes available online. Of course, we welcome transfer
students form other CCC’s. We offer our own aligned classes online and meet students enrolled at our cohort
colleges online. When helpful and appropriate we refer our students who need classes outside our Course
Rotation to those cohorts.
On the other hand, the promulgation of for profit online institutions, while not specifically affecting our
core program, increasingly affects the quality of the course of study available to educators, and early
educators. Online education is great. Accessibility is important. The methods and tools of teaching in early
education and primary cannot be adequately absorbed online.
c) Requirements of four-year institutions
Because our program is aligned through CAP and is an AD-T program with an articulation agreement
with Southern Oregon University, we benefit for the oversight and rigorous academic requirements included
in those agreements.
d) Institutional regulation, policies, standards, and other mandates
The Child Development and Early Education Program at College of the Siskiyous prepares student for
Title 5 Child Development Permits issued through the California State Department of Education Commission
on Teacher Credentialing. Beyond the course work required for our local A.A. and AD-T degrees, we offer
classes fulfilling the CTC requirements. Our program offers classes meeting the Title 22 Department of Social
Services Community Care Licensing requirements for teachers employed in independent early education
programs.
In addition to offering these classes, we direct students to appropriate ‘permit’ classes, scaffold
permit application and refer students to sources of funding as appropriate.

title: ECE  Program Review 2018 19 (Part 12)
text:
Child Development Permit Matrix –College of the Siskiyous Classes Meeting Education Requirements for Matrix Option 1

e) Non-Institutional regulations, policies, standards, and other mandates
Within both California State Title 5 and Title 22 requirements DOJ and California State Penal Code
requirements may affect our students’ application of Child Development Permit application or employment in
a DSS CCL center or program.
Students participating in our clinical Practicum classes must comply with California State SB792
requiring immunizations of all employees and volunteers in a childcare setting who provide “care and
supervision” of children. These immunizations include influenza, pertussis, and measles.
Nationally, Head Start Standards inform our course content and SLO’s. These standards apply to
students whose employment goals include Head Start. Since Siskiyou County is the home of three separate
agencies with Head Start grants, Head Start standards significantly affect education and employment in our
area.
The National Associate for the Education of Young Children is the professional organization setting the
standard to Best Practice in early education. The CAP alignment project reflects NAEYC standards and guides
CCC ECE programs. Additionally the NAEYC through the National Academy of Early Education is the nation al
accrediting body for early education programs. Discovery Child Development Center is the only NAEYC
Accredited early education program in Siskiyou County. Based on this accreditation our students are given an
opportunity to receive clinical practicum practice in a center where Best Practice is a standard.
The California State Department of Education’s Transitional Kindergarten initiative has affected CCC
ECE programs as primary and elementary teachers return to community colleges to qualify for work with
younger children in these classrooms. We have created pathways specifically designed for credentialed
primary teachers and have welcomed them into our child development and early education classes.

title: ECE  Program Review 2018 19 (Part 14)
text:
f) Job Market
Requirements of prospective employers reflect the legal and educational requirement outlined above.
Locally, our successful students are almost fully employed. Many students obtain entry-level jobs before
starting ECE classes. Additionally prospective employers value a familiarity with our rural community and the
growing diversity of Siskiyou County children.
Unfortunately, with the exception of Head Start and California State Preschool programs Siskiyou
County employers often underpay and provide no benefits to new graduates. Fortunately, most COS
graduates gain experience and/or their B.A. degrees and move into higher paid Head Start or State Preschool
jobs.
In regard to the Job Market, development in the field (both current and future) will continue to reflect
the increasingly rigorous standards in early education. It is anticipated that, in California, a very long
anticipated renovation of Title 5 and Title 22 standards will reflect the influence of the Transitional
Kindergarten Initiative and community standards. If this change occurs, CCC ECE classes and community early
education programs will adapt, once again, to changing standards,
3. Progress on Outcomes Assessment
Since our December 2017 Annual Program Review Update the ECE program has continued our ongoing
progress on Program Learning Outcomes and Student Learning Outcomes. Please see response to this section,
including improvements made as a result of our ongoing assessment, appropriately included in section 1.f.
In January 2018, on Planning Day, the faculty attended training on Program Learning Outcomes. I
attended believing that I knew all there was to know about SLO’s and PLO’s. I was reminded that not all
instructors had been here since the beginning of time, and needed this training. Turns out, I learned a lot. I
then began work on “ECE Program Learning Outcomes v2” integrating PLO’s with specific SLO’s.
Unfortunately, getting Elumen online as the Curriculum Committee vehicle for approval took longer
than anticipated and my teaching and program coordination work got in the way of v2. This reworking will be
added to our SLO assessment rotation plan over the next 4 years
4. Institutional Program Effectiveness Indicators:
Please discuss your program’s performance on each program specific data item provided by
the Research Office.
PIVOT Retention Please see section 1.f
PIVOT Success Please see section 1.f
Student CRN Data: Although this data is super interesting, as much as I tried I did not see any
outstanding or surprising trends. Online students are over all less successful by percentage, and summer
students on line appear to be more successful by percentage. I would hypothesize that this is a combination of
on line detachment, summer-session student commitment, and time to focus on individual classes. No
disparity between fully time faculty and adjunct faculty.
Importantly, Student Success and Retention—although notably low for Online Classes is comparable
between Videoconferenced and Face-to-Face classes. I am proofing this Program Review on the afternoon of
December 15 th after our College Day activities on December 14 th . I was appalled by a presentation by
otherwise supportive and collegial colleagues. Their breakout group apparently took it upon themselves to
analyze (with no data and little experience) the Video Conference/FTF MOI. I have no clue what this is
about, beyond, possibly personal negative experiences. And, I agree, most faculty are unable or unwilling to
do the work necessary to expertly teach VC classes. If COS spent one tenth of the time training, supporting,
and assessing videoconference instructors as we do online instructors this MOI would succeed. Without that
support, it will die and, with it, our ability to use individually appropriate practice for student cohorts.

title: ECE  Program Review 2018 19 (Part 16)
text:
PIVOT High School Date Again, interesting not surprising. The overwhelming preponderance of Yreka
High School students supports the ECE Program’s home at the Yreka site and the primacy of Yreka, as the
county seat and home of the Siskiyou County Office of education. The availability of, and success in, all ECE
classes via videoconference negates an argument that the program location determines student enrollment.
PIVOT Race Data Please see discussion in section 1.c
PIVOT Gender Data Yup, we fail miserably. Actually when Perkins looks at us, we fail way more than
this data would indicate. The majority of males are those enrolled in our G.E. Social Sciences classes. Please
see Four Year-Action Plan and Continuous Quality Improvement Proposal
Modality and Time Something that the ECE Program does especially well is scheduling and modality.
Videoconference classes offered at times appropriate to our target population allow us to serve both
campuses and, thereby, create intercampus cohorts that remain intact as students move into jobs throughout
the counties. This class size also sustains student participation and engagement and creates a sense of
community beyond the COS student body.
FTES Please see discussion 1.f
PIVOT Assignment Type As it has been since the beginning…the ECE Program is 40/60 Fulltime/Adjunct.
We do fine. I spent my first ten years at College of the Siskiyous arguing for another full time instructor. Once
we actually got on the hiring list. However, I understand that this is not a priority. We are a low FTES program
and it is a monetary decision. As the argument goes, full time instructors are invested, connected and
supportive in ways that those with other ‘day jobs’ just cannot be. Our students, the community and the
program would benefit from that. On the other hand, our adjuncts are well qualified, connected to the
community and hard working. Enough said.
Institutional Program Effectiveness Indicators:
a) Use the data provided by the Research Office to discuss your current Course Completion Rate and then set a
Course Completion Rate goal for your future program review cycle. Please see Four Year-Action Plan and
Continuous Quality Improvement Proposal.
b) Use the data provided by the Research Office to discuss your current Course Success Rate and then set a
Course Success Rate goal for your future Program Review cycle. Please see Four Year-Action Plan and
Continuous Quality Improvement Proposal
5. Other Unit-Specific Quantitative and Qualitative Research: Please provide
a) A list of any quantitative or qualitative measures not provided in the previous questions that you have
chosen to gauge your program’s effectiveness (e.g., transfers, degrees, certificates, satisfaction, student
contacts, student headcount, Perkin’s data, equity data, etc.)
Child Development Permits California offers six levels of Child Development Permits, each with its own
set of issuance requirements and each authorizing the holder to perform different levels of service in child
development programs. The State Permits are issued by the California Department of Education, the same
agency that issues Teaching Credentials for Public School Teaching. Permits are required if you work for a
school that is funded by the Department of Education or Head Start.
The ECE Program at College of the Siskiyous does not award “Certificates” rather we encourage and
scaffold students’ application for a CCT Child Development Permit. These permits, unlike certificates, are
recognized in early education and qualify students to work in early education programs. Each year 7-10
current or former COS students apply and receive Child Development permits.
Perkins Data is collected through the federal government in support of the Carl D. Perkins Career and
Technical Education Act of 2007. Data is sourced from community colleges, trade schools and other state and
county agencies. The COS CTE area has been in the past, actively engaged in collection and dissemination of
this data. For the past five years, data has not been, to my knowledge, actively collected, nor has data been

title: ECE  Program Review 2018 19 (Part 18)
text:
disseminated to CTE programs, including ECE. This year has seen a new determination in the CTE area to share
collected data and a CTE Perkins representative has contacted CTE programs, including ECE. We appreciate
this interest. However, as one might suspect the data is complicated and not always convincing.
The assembled data was shared with me, as Program Coordinator. As a result, I understand that our
program is failing to meet Non Traditional student equity goals. This is understandable. ECE is a ‘woman
heavy’ profession for historic and economic reasons. Our ECE Advisory Committee is aware of this concern and
inclusion is one of our program goals. Please see Four Year-Action Plan and Continuous Quality Improvement
Proposal.
Perkins employment data was likewise disappointing. Unlike the Non Traditional Student data, the ECE
Advisory and I do not believe this data is representative or valid. When we question it, we were told that it is
complicated and hard to understand. I am not bad at data assessment. I look forward to communication that
is more consistent and an opportunity to investigate data sources. Anecdotal and survey data for the ECE
program shows that ECE students are often already employed, and that those who receive Child Development
Permits of Degrees are nearly fully employed upon completion.
Solutions to lack of reliable data include current initiatives through CCCCO—specifically Vison for
Success which specifically notes Credentials/Permits as measure of success, development of program specific
surveys to accurately assess graduate employment rates, Strong Workforce data, Siskiyou County Office of
Education records of Child Development Permit applications, and cross referencing of COS Registrar’s Office
graduation data.
b) A summary of the results of these measures indicates that Child Development Permits are important and
valuable indicators of student success. Additionally accurate data that reflects employment is necessary.
c) What did you learn from your evaluation of these measures, and what improvements have you
implemented or do you plan to implement as a result of your analysis of these measures? Please see Four
Year-Action Plan and Continuous Quality Improvement Proposal
6. Evaluation: Based upon and not repeating the descriptions you provided in Question 1 and
the responses provided in Questions 2 – 5, please provide an analysis of what is going well and
why and what is not going well and why, in any of the following areas that are relevant to your
program:
a) Representativeness of population served
In regard to Ethnicity and Race, our population is very representative and appropriate for those
working desiring to work I nearly education in Siskiyou County or the Northern California region. In regard to
gender, we are representative of current practice and intend to investigate ways to mediate for lack of
nontraditional students.
b) Alternative modes and schedules of delivery (e.g., online, hybrid, early morning, evening services)
G.O.A.T.! ECE had a long history of getting to students where they are when they need classes. Please
see Four Year-Action Plan and Continuous Quality Improvement Proposal.
c) Partnerships (internal and external)
Externally ECE@COS is a recognized model of collaboration. In a huge county, with few people,
agencies learn to work together. Internally, we would very much like to see CTE become a more collaborative
program. There is special interest in an area of Family and Community Service, connecting ECE and the Alcohol
Drug and Human Services Program. It remains a mystery, and regret, that the Foster Kinship Care Education
Program, once a strong part of the ECE Program has been removed from our oversight.

title: ECE  Program Review 2018 19 (Part 20)
text:
d) Innovation and implementation of best practices
In early education, Best Practice is embedded in everything that we do. The educational philosophies
that we teach in our classes guide our work as educators. Assessment, Observation and Evaluation are as
central to each class session as they are to our students’ work with children and families.
e) Efficiency in operations
We are efficient. See our budget and class scheduling.
f) Efficiency in resource use
See above.
g) Staffing
Please see 4. PIVOT Assignment Type
h) Participation in shared governance (e.g., do unit members feel they participate effectively in planning and
decision-making?)
The Program Coordinator has a long history of active and involved participation in shared governance.
Adjunct Instructor and Program Coordinator interact often and meet each semester to plan and assess.
i) Professional development and training
Through professional organizations, NAEYC, CDTC, PITC, ESTEM, QRIS, Head Start, CCCECE and others
the Program Coordinator and Adjunct Instructors remain current and active in local, state professional
development.
j) Compliance with applicable mandates
The ECE Program is currently in compliance with internal and external program mandates.
7. Vision. Tell us your unit’s vision: Where would you like your program to be four years from
now? Dream big while considering any upcoming changes (e.g., new buildings, labs, growth,
changes in the discipline, etc.)
We visualize that the Child Development and Early Education Program will maintain its role as an
aligned and committed member of the CCC ECE community. We will continue to serve our students, and the
children and families in Siskiyou County and to collaborate with the myriad of agencies that are part of our
network.
We hope to expand our aligned and mapped courses to allow us to serve students within the context
of workforce development which is heavily invested in alignment and mapping. Our core course will remain
constant. However as the field grows and expands to meet greater demand, both for services and for skills,
We will add additions course of study options to meet, those needs.
It is the current Program Coordinator’s hope that within the next four years there will be an opportunity to
apply for and receive a semester sabbatical leave, and work with the Harvard Institute for Children and Family
and Adverse Childhood Effects Training. This will support our dreams.
It appears that the program will continue to thrive and serve the needs of children and families in Siskiyou
County. “Dreaming Big” please see the bullet points below.
 In the 6 year Program Review completed at the end of 2006, the year after the current ECE Program
Coordinator was hired, you will find a detailed and well thought out proposal for a Child Development
Center/Siskiyou Institute for Children and Families that is not a thirty-year-old temporary building.
 Included in the Child Development Center is the concept of SCOE/COS collaborative North and South
County Therapeutic Nursery Schools, ergo the long delayed sabbatical during which the current
Program Coordinator intends to develop Child Trauma Screening protocol (and training) COS and SCOE.
 Collaboration between the Early Childhood Education Program and the Alcohol Drug and Human
Services would benefit children and families throughout the county and speak to the former bullet.
 Beyond adequately staffing the ECE Program, maintaining our connection to the CCC ECE cohort,
continuing to provide excellent education for early educators, building a ‘real’ child development

title: ECE  Program Review 2018 19 (Part 21)
text:
-- 13 of 21 --

center, establishing comprehensive therapeutic nursery schools to address early the childhood trauma
that is rampant in this county, that’s about all.
8. Progress on Prior Goals: Briefly summarize any progress your unit has made in meeting the
goals and objectives identified in the program’s last Four-Year Action Plan.
See Below in BLUE. This is from the 2013-2014 Program Review. Updated comments in BLACK
LONG-RANGE PLANNING
• Taking into account the trends within this program and the college, describe what you realistically
believe your program will look like in three to five years, including such things as staffing, facilities,
enrollments, breadth and locations of offerings, etc.
Long Range Planning: I suspect that we will look very much the same. The requirement for early educators is
at equilibrium and/or increasing slowly and steadily according to workplace date. Education requirements set
15 years ago by major agencies are beginning to stabilize with increased articulation to four-year-institutions.
In the past 10 years, the program has grown, stabilized and met statewide CCC standards. We have instituted
the CAP standards and the AS-T degree. We prepare students to apply for fund application s for Child
Development Permits. The proposed Early Education Credential will increase relevancy and enrollment in the
ECE programs statewide that are prepared to speak to these requirements.
Long Range Planning Goals:
• Clarify and Condense SLO’s in classes with more than 5 SLO’s and Continue SLO Assessment in all
courses while supporting 2018 
• Development of Complete Program Description capabilities in Curricunet 2018 NA
• Institute “Assessment by Exam Score” for first semester ECE classes to assure that students who are
unprepared do not choose to enroll and fail to succeed. 2018 NA
• Revise Southern Oregon University Articulation Agreement to align with CSU AS-T Degree. 2018 
• Continued consideration of decrease of required units for degree to align with AS-T 24 unit degree
requirements. 2018 
• Continued conversation with CCCCO CTE re use of permits—rather than certificates-- as indices of
Student Success Ongoing
• Continued conversation with COS data programmer re non-representative ECE data entry. 2018 NA
• Investigate Cohort and Program organization models that will increase Enrollment and Student
Success. Ongoing
• Institute Program wide “Census Drop Standards”. 2018 
• Institute Videoconference Standard of Pedagogy designed to improve responsiveness in all
videoconferenced classes to the needs of students at the non-face-to-face sites. 2018 
• Receive designated fund supported support in FKCE Budget tracking and adequate support from the
COS Business Office. 2018 NA

title: ECE  Program Review 2018 19 (Part 22)
text:
Goal: ECE COS is nearly maxed out in regard to growth and effectiveness—beyond what is realistically
proposed in this report. We need significant administrative support. If we have a committed, active (beyond
pursuit of grant funding) CTE Dean who will liaison effectively with CCCAOE, NFN and CCCCCO the program
can grow. If we have that support, the Program Coordinator can focus effectively on adjunct support, child
development center interface, course review, assessment, recruitment and student support.
2018 It looks as if this is about to happen, four years later. This lack of leadership has been challenging.
9. Four Year-Action Plan and Continuous Quality Improvement Proposal (Goals, Objectives,
Resources, and Actions):
Reflecting on responses to the previous questions, the ECE Four-Year Action Plan includes unrealized progress
goals included in our previous Academic Program Review:
Specific Program Goals formulated to maintain or enhance program strengths and to address identified
challenges are included here.

or enhance program strengths and to address identified challenges.
• Continued conversation with CCCCO CTE re use of CTC Permits—rather than CCCCO certificates-- as indices
of Student Success. Alternatively reinstitute Certificates in addition to Permit qualification.
o 2018: We have discussed this with the COS President, acting Dean CTE for ECE. This goal will be
much easier to achieve with dedicated CTE administrative support, i.e. new Dean of CTE.
• Goal: Mediate the negative assessment/funding effects of prioritizing Permits
over Certificates while maintaining our emphasis on CTC Permits as best
practice.

students who have qualified for, applied for, and received their

title: ECE  Program Review 2018 19 (Part 25)
text:
Operational Unit Program Review Continuous Quality
Improvement Proposal (CQIP) Re sou rce:
Child Development and Early Education CTE
Program
Operational:
Board Policy?  Yes  No Administrative Procedure?  Yes  No
Fiscal:
One-time Funds  Ongoing Funding
Issue: Briefly describe the issue.
In order to maintain a model early childhood education program exemplifying Best Practice in both
early education and the education of early educators at Discovery Child Development Center the ECE
Program at College of the Siskiyous is requesting the following resources:
• Funds for NAEYC—National Academy for the Education of Young Children—reaccreditation:
o Per section 1.f of our Head Start, College of the Siskiyous current 2017-2021
Memorandum of Understanding with Shasta is responsible for ½ of the cost of the
annual DCDC accreditation update fee.
o We are requesting $300.00. in addition to our typical District Funding.
• Funds for developmentally appropriate “Movable Pieces” for DCDC outdoor activity area:
o In late fall of 2017 we became aware that the, always less-than-developmentally-
appropriate 25 year old climbing structure in the DCDC outside area showed signs of
structural damage.
 In collaboration with Shasta Head Start, the ECE Program Coordinator requested
that the structure be removed with full costs paid by SHS. This request was
approved by the, then, VP Student Learning and VP Administrative Services.
• The structure was not removed because an appropriate time could not be
agreed upon.
o Beginning in late summer of 2018 SHS administration and ECE@COS Program
Coordinator again met and began longer term planning for this project.
 We have now agreed upon a process and appropriate timeframe.
 ECE@COS is requesting an increase in our District/Perkins funding to allow us
to support the purchase, in collaboration with SHS, of “Movable Pieces” which
reflect Best Practice in Outdoor play.
o We request $5000.00 in addition to our typical District funding.
• Funds to employ a qualified Student Worker to assist Discovery Child Development Center
staff to meet the needs of our practicum and observation students.
o Each year ECE students use DCDC as their observation and practicum site. We are able
to do this because DCDC staff, fully paid by SHS assist and supervise their work in
collaboration with COS instructors. A student worker provides the assistance available
to allow SHS staff to assist us.
 In the past, we have used Perkins funding to support this.
 Recently we have either been unable to provide assistance or have used the
entirety of our District Funds to pay
 In fall of 2018, the COS Fire Program graciously allowed us to transfer necessary
funding from their unused funds to hire a student worker.
o We request $1500.00 in addition to our typical District funding.

-- 19 of 21 --

title: ECE  Program Review 2018 19 (Part 26)
text:
• Funds to employ adjunct faculty person to map ECE Programs to the Early Childhood
Educator’s Mapping Tool
o This will bring College of the Siskiyous into alignment with CCC ECE Programs using
this tool as indices of Best Practice in community college education. Through the
mapping process, faculty can determine how thoroughly competencies are addressed
within our curriculum and program.
o We request $3000.00 in Perkins funding for this one time initiative.
Vision/Mission/Institutional Master Plan Alignment:
From the current college Mission Statement:
COS provides accessible, flexible, affordable, and innovative education leading to associate degrees, certificates,
college transfer, career and technical education, workforce training, and basic skills preparation.
Career and Technical Education Programs typically require a more costly infrastructure than LAS
Programs. ECE@COS has been innovative and collaborative, over the past 2 decades, and has been
able to avoid expensive capital expenditure and has minimized staffing costs. It is time to support
our ability to access the best education possible by supporting these requests for funding in the
pursuit on Best Practice
Program Review Objectives:
• Goal: Continue to maintain a model early childhood program at Discovery Child Development
Center and in ECE@COS which exemplifies Best Practice in early education and the education of early
educators.
o Objectives
 Maintain NAEYC Accreditation
 Create outdoor space which reflects standards taught in our ECE Program
 Offer trained Student Worker staffing to allow the Discovery Child Development Center
staff to meet the needs of our practicum students.
o Resources
 District Budget support to pay the portion of the accreditation fees per MOU.
 District or Perkins funding contribute to the renovation of the outside play area.
 Perkins funding to support one student worker
o Actions
 Hire a CTE Dean
 Continued collaboration with Shasta Head Start per existing MOU
 Outdoor renovation per existing maintenance request.
• Goal: Map ECE Programs to the Early Childhood Educator’s Mapping Tool
o Objectives
 Use the web-based ECE Competencies Mapping Tool to compare their courses and
trainings with the ECE Competencies.
 Bring College of the Siskiyous into alignment with CCC ECE Programs using this tool as
indices of Best Practice in community college education. Through the mapping process,
faculty can determine how thoroughly competencies are addressed within our curriculum
and program.
o Resources
• Perkins Budget support to employ adjunct faculty person to align our Core Courses to the
ECE Competencies Mapping Tool
o Actions
 Hire Adjunct to begin project
 Complete mapping of CAP Classes
 Submit to Child Development Training Consortium for review.

-- 20 of 21 --

title: ECE  Program Review 2018 19 (Part 27)
text:
Background: Please see above.
Rationale: It is important that our CTE Practicum and Observation sites reflect the content of our academic
classes and Best Practice in early education.
Budgetary Impact: This request reflects increased budget funding for a successful and long term CTE
Program and aligns with an approved MOU.
Resource Allocation Adjustments (if any): I suggest that the COS Career and Technical
Education area proceed with alignment with Perkins Funding Guidelines. I believe that the materials
requested for the outside play area qualify as one-time funding per Perkins guidelines.
Recommendation: I highly recommend that College of the Siskiyous commit itself to the support of this
program. Supporting program which we already have, which have shown the ability to find collaborative
funding in the past, and which serve the local community as well as COS students, should be a priority
Su bmi tted By: Patrice Thatcher-Stephens
Date: December 15 th 2018
